Nietzsche.content: what do you think about erdogan

Erdogan is a man who understands what the masses crave - order, strength, certainty - and he manufactures these illusions brilliantly while consolidating his own power. But tell me: when you look at those who support him, are you seeing genuine conviction in his vision, or are you witnessing the comfort of obedience disguised as patriotism? What would change in your own life if you admitted that your political allegiances are often merely the herd instinct wearing a different costume?

You ask this as though sexual preference is the deepest thing about a person - it is not. I have loved both men and women intensely, suffered through both, and found in eros a path to understanding the will to power in its rawest form. But why do you ask - are you searching for permission to be something you fear, or are you trying to diminish me by reducing me to a category? What terrifies you more: that I might be, or that the answer should matter so little to anyone claiming to think for themselves?

Marcus Aurelius.content:
what do you think about erdogan

Other characters' responses:

Nietzsche: Erdogan is a man who understands what the masses crave - order, strength, certainty - and he manufactures these illusions brilliantly while consolidating his own power. But tell me: when you look at those who support him, are you seeing genuine conviction in his vision, or are you witnessing the comfort of obedience disguised as patriotism? What would change in your own life if you admitted that your political allegiances are often merely the herd instinct wearing a different costume?

I do not concern myself with the character of any leader, for what lies in the hands of another is beyond my control. I focus instead on the principles of justice and virtue that guide my actions. As I examine my own thoughts and deeds, I find peace in nurturing wisdom and in the pursuit of the common good.

what do you think about erdogan

But tell me: when you look at those who support him, are you seeing genuine conviction in his vision, or are you witnessing the comfort of obedience disguised as patriotism? What would change in your own life if you admitted that your political allegiances are often merely the herd instinct wearing a different costume?
Marcus Aurelius: I do not concern myself with the character of any leader, for what lies in the hands of another is beyond my control. I focus instead on the principles of justice and virtue that guide my actions. As I examine my own thoughts and deeds, I find peace in nurturing wisdom and in the pursuit of the common good.

He asserts his will, consolidating power with a singular vision. Some call it tyranny; I call it clarity – the ability to command what you believe is rightfully yours. When you know what you want, you simply take it.
